name: journalctl
context: Query, tail, filter, export, and maintain structured logs stored in the systemd journal
info: This skill covers `journalctl`, the primary interface to logs stored by journald. It includes unit-focused inspection with `--unit`, boot-aware debugging with `--boot`, filtering by time, priority, identifier, grep pattern, or structured `FIELD=VALUE` matches, and output modes ranging from concise text to JSON and export streams. It also covers maintenance operations such as disk-usage checks, verification, rotation, flushing, and vacuuming old journal files. Agents use this skill to diagnose failed services, compare current and previous boots, extract machine-readable logs, and keep journal storage under control.
triggers: show logs for a systemd service
body:
# journalctl

journald stores structured logs from system and user services. `journalctl` can read by unit, boot, time range, priority, or arbitrary `FIELD=VALUE` matches. For service debugging, start narrow with `-u UNIT -n 100 --no-pager` and expand only when needed.

```bash
journalctl -u my-app.service -n 200 --no-pager
journalctl -u my-app.service -b -1 -p err..alert --no-pager
journalctl --since '2026-04-11 12:00:00' --until '2026-04-11 13:00:00' -o short-full
journalctl -u my-app.service -o json --no-pager
journalctl -u my-app.service -f
```

Maintenance commands are part of the same CLI:

```bash
journalctl --disk-usage
journalctl --rotate
journalctl --vacuum-time=14d
journalctl --verify
```

Permissions matter. By default, users can read their own journals, but full system journals are usually limited to root or users in groups such as `systemd-journal`, `adm`, or `wheel`. Pair this skill with [[systemd/systemctl]] to identify the unit and [[systemd/troubleshooting#failed-service]] when logs explain a startup failure.

sections.title: Filters
sections.body: The highest-value filters are `--unit=`, `--boot=`, `--since=`, `--until=`, `--priority=`, and `--grep=`. Structured matching also works through raw fields such as `_SYSTEMD_UNIT=my-app.service` or `SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER=my-app`. Multiple different fields are ANDed together; multiple matches on the same field are ORed.

sections.title: Output modes
sections.body: Use `short-iso` or `short-full` for readable timestamps, `with-unit` when instance names matter, `json` for machine processing, and `export` for backup or transport-oriented streams. `-f` follows new entries, `-n` limits lines, and `-r` shows newest entries first.

sections.title: Maintenance
sections.body: `journalctl --disk-usage` reports total storage, `--rotate` forces a new journal file, `--vacuum-time=`, `--vacuum-size=`, and `--vacuum-files=` trim retention, and `--verify` checks consistency. Use `--flush` to move runtime data from `/run` into `/var` when persistent storage is configured.

sections.title: Permissions and scope
sections.body: Use `--system` for system logs and `--user` for the current user's journal. Reading the full system journal generally requires root or membership in privileged log-reading groups. For a failing service, check that you are querying the same scope the unit runs in before concluding that logs are missing.

scripts.name: Journal Operations
scripts.description: Run filtered journal queries and maintenance actions such as vacuum, rotate, and verify

SYSTEMD_JOURNAL_ACTION.description: Action such as query, disk-usage, verify, vacuum-time, rotate, or list-boots

SYSTEMD_JOURNAL_SCOPE.description: Journal scope: all, system, or user

SYSTEMD_JOURNAL_PRIORITY.description: Priority or range such as err..alert

SYSTEMD_JOURNAL_OUTPUT.description: Output mode such as short-full, json, or export

SYSTEMD_JOURNAL_LINES.description: Line limit for queries or follow mode

output.description: Output mode such as short-full or json

depends.reason: The wrapper is a bash script that invokes journalctl
